kurma-stotram: When Vishnu took the form of the great tortoise to steady Mount Mandara during the churning of the cosmic ocean, he became the very ground beneath creation's turning. The Kurma Stotram — श्री कूर्म स्तोत्रम् — draws on that image of the Kurma avatar to ask for stability and support through life's own churning moments. Eleven verses from the Kurma Purana, roughly eight minutes spoken aloud. Tradition holds that sincere recitation washes away accumulated sins, surrounds the devotee with divine protection, and opens the way to brahma-jnana, the supreme knowledge. Many keep it for Thursday worship and for Kurma Jayanti. It grants, above all, a grounded footing for spiritual practice — and the promise of vishnuloka after death.
kushmanda-stotram: On the fourth night of Navratri the Mother is worshipped as Kushmanda, she whose faint smile is said to have produced the cosmic egg and kindled the first light. The कूष्माण्डा स्तोत्रम् sings to this aspect — vitality radiant as the sun, the prana-shakti that quickens every living body. Devotees turn to it for radiant health and for the heart's opening, the Anahata chakra stirred toward love and compassion. Ten verses, about five minutes. Recited faithfully, it is believed to confer all eight siddhis and a share in the Goddess's own power to create and sustain. Tuesdays, Fridays and Sundays suit it well, with the fourth day of Navratri being its proper occasion. Part of the Navdurga stotra cycle.
lakshmi-ashtakam: Eight verses, eight forms of fortune. The Lakshmi Ashtakam — लक्ष्मी अष्टकम् — addresses Mahalakshmi in her completeness, calling down the ashta-lakshmi, the eightfold wealth that ranges from grain and gold to courage and progeny. Older practitioners recommend reciting it three times daily; done so, it is said to clear away enemies, while a single devoted reading lifts the weight of sins and negative karma. The hymn speaks plainly to two ends at once — material abundance here, and liberation beyond it. Friday is its day, and Diwali its festival, when poverty is asked to leave the house for good. About eight minutes to recite. Above all it is offered simply to please the Goddess and earn her lasting grace.
lakshmi-chalisa: A chalisa takes its name from the chalis — forty — chaupais at its core, with framing dohas rounding the Lakshmi Chalisa (लक्ष्मी चालीसा) to its full count of 42 verse-units. Set aside about fifteen minutes for it. The verses praise the Goddess of fortune and ask, in homely Hindi, for the things a household actually needs: wealth that flows without interruption, the steady good luck that keeps a family afloat, and peace under one roof. Where there is poverty or financial strain, the Chalisa is recited to dissolve it. Devotees fold it into Friday worship and Diwali, trusting it to carry both material and spiritual wishes to fulfilment under the divine grace of Lakshmi herself.
lakshmi-dhyanam: Before you ask Lakshmi for anything, you first picture her — seated on the lotus, lotus in hand, gold and grace pouring from her. The लक्ष्मी ध्यानम् gathers the classic meditation shlokas that build exactly this inner image, including the well-loved 'Nityam Sa Padmahasta' verse invoked for household prosperity, and the Lakshmi bija mantra for those who want to go deeper into dhyana. Seven verses, about four minutes, drawn from the Lakshmi Tantra and Pancharatra Agama. It calls down her grace for wealth, beauty and auspiciousness. A natural fit for Friday puja, for Diwali, Lakshmi Puja and Sharad Purnima — or simply for daily worship at the home shrine.
lakshmi-hridayam: Hridayam means heart, and this hymn claims to hold the very heart-essence of Lakshmi's grace. The Lakshmi Hridaya Stotram (लक्ष्मी हृदय स्तोत्रम्) belongs to the Atharva Veda's Khila Sukta tradition and runs to fifteen verses, around ten minutes. Its promise is large and specific: recited three times a day, it is said to fulfil every desire without exception, while purifying all sins and granting supreme merit. The deeper assurance, though, is that once invoked Lakshmi never leaves the devotee's household — she settles in, bringing progeny, wealth, health, fame, courage and good fortune in her wake. Where poverty lingers, the stotra is the traditional remedy. Friday is its day; Diwali and Varalakshmi Vrata its high occasions.
lakshmi-narasimha-karavalamba-stotram: Karavalamba — the supporting hand. Every one of these sixteen verses is a man overboard in the sea of samsara, crying out for Narasimha to reach down and pull him up. Adi Shankaracharya wrote it, and it remains among his most beloved compositions. The लक्ष्मीनृसिंह करावलम्ब स्तोत्रम् braids together the ferocity of the man-lion and the softening grace of Lakshmi, which is why it is reckoned so fierce a shield against danger of every kind — including the fear of death, disease, and the messengers of Yama. Relief from worldly suffering comes quickly to those who mean it. Recited on Thursdays, Saturdays and Sundays, and on Narasimha Jayanti, its end is the Lord's lotus feet: moksha. About twelve minutes.
lakshmi-narayana-hridayam: Some devotees keep two heart-hymns; this one folds both into a single recitation. The लक्ष्मीनारायण हृदयम् weaves the Narayana Hridayam and the Lakshmi Hridayam together so that the divine couple is addressed at once, their graces arriving inseparable. Thirteen verses from the Atharva Veda's Khila Sukta tradition, about twelve minutes. Read each morning, it is said to fulfil every desire and to establish Lakshmi's permanent presence in the home and family — she comes and does not depart. What it asks for is comprehensive: wealth, progeny, strength, radiance, fame, longevity and health, the worldly gifts set beside spiritual liberation. Friday and Saturday are its days; Diwali and Ekadashi its festivals.
lakshmi-narayana-stotram: There is a logic to praying to Lakshmi and Narayana together rather than apart — fortune and its keeper, the wife and the husband, asked in one breath for blessings that complete each other. That is the spirit of the लक्ष्मी नारायण स्तोत्रम्, a nine-verse hymn from Vaishnavite tradition, about eight minutes to recite. It is offered for wealth and abundance, for the lifting of poverty and the small troubles that wear down a household, and for harmony in married life. Couples find in it a prayer for togetherness; everyone finds in it the joining of material and spiritual fulfilment. Devotees take it up on Thursdays and Fridays, and on Ekadashi, Diwali and Kartik Purnima — though it sits comfortably in any morning's worship of the divine pair.
lakshmi-sahasranama: A thousand names of the Goddess of fortune, gathered from the Skanda Purana and arranged across thirty verses — set aside half an hour for the full recitation. The लक्ष्मी सहस्रनाम स्तोत्रम् is the most expansive of the Lakshmi hymns, summoning all eight of her forms, the Ashta-Lakshmi, at a single sitting. Where the shorter prayers ask for a measure of grace, this one asks for all-round prosperity: permanent wealth, fortune, financial stability, and the kind of standing that does not slip. It is the classical remedy for poverty, debt and hardship, said to clear them completely, and it carries blessings of progeny, family happiness and marital bliss. Recited on Fridays and at Diwali — also Navratri and Varalakshmi Vrata — its fruits are said to multiply.
lakshmi-stuti: When the gods churned the ocean and Lakshmi rose from it, it was Indra himself, king of the devas, who broke into praise — and his words became this Stuti. Preserved in the Vishnu Purana as the Indrakrita Stuti, it counts among the most authentic of all Lakshmi prayers. The लक्ष्मी स्तुति names her as Sri, Ishvari, Vidya, Medha — fortune, sovereignty, learning, intelligence — every good quality folded into one Goddess. Ten verses, about five minutes. Its central petition is sarva sampat samriddhi, complete prosperity without reservation, the lifting of poverty and the bestowing of wealth in all its forms. Diwali, Fridays and Varalakshmi Vrata are its natural occasions.
lalita-trishati: Lord Hayagriva taught it to the sage Agastya as the highest form of devotion, and tradition guards it as more secret and more potent even than the Lalita Sahasranama. The ललिता त्रिशती स्तोत्रम् sets out 300 names of Lalita Tripurasundari, drawn from the Lalitopakhyana of the Brahmanda Purana — twenty verses, about twenty minutes. To recite it is to ask for all four aims of human life at once: dharma, artha, kama and moksha. It is said to dissolve sins and karmic knots carried over from past lives, and to grant beauty, grace, and a spiritual magnetism that is hard to name and harder to ignore. Tuesdays and Fridays suit it; Navratri and Lalita Jayanti are its festivals.
laxminarayan-chalisa: Forty chaupais give the chalisa its name; with the framing dohas the Laxminarayan Chalisa (लक्ष्मीनारायण चालीसा) comes to 42 verse-units, roughly fifteen minutes spoken. It addresses Lakshmi and Narayana as a pair, and reads like a householder's wish-list set to verse — wealth and abundance settling into the home, poverty and financial strain pushed out for good, the slow lift of success in business and career. For married couples it asks plainly for love, harmony and togetherness. Its high day is the Deepawali Lakshmi-Narayan Puja, when the divine couple is worshipped side by side, and devotees also keep it for Thursdays, Fridays and Devuthani Ekadashi.
lingashtakam: Eight verses, each circling back to the Shiva linga itself — the formless made worship-able. The लिङ्गाष्टकम् offers praise to the linga that the temple priests pour milk and water over, and recitation is counted equal in merit to that very abhishekam, which is why many keep it on their lips through the bathing rite. About five minutes to chant. It is said to burn away sins gathered across many lifetimes, to drive out the eight kinds of poverty, the ashta daridra, and to thin the pride, ego and arrogance that cloud a person. It sharpens intellect and wisdom, and promises Shivaloka to the one who departs. Mondays are its day, with Maha Shivaratri and the month of Shravan its seasons.
maha-ganapati-stotram: This is Ganesha in his tantric grandeur — Maha Ganapati, the ten-armed form worshipped where Vedic and Tantric streams meet. The महा गणपति स्तोत्रम्, eight verses long and about seven minutes to recite, comes out of the tantric tradition and reaches past the everyday removal of small hindrances toward the clearing of major obstacles, the kind that block a whole life or a long sadhana. Reciters seek through it the siddhis, the spiritual accomplishments that practice is meant to ripen, and success in the inner work itself. Wednesday is the day for it; Ganesh Chaturthi the festival when the form is most fully honoured.
maha-shivratri-stotram: Maha Shivaratri is kept as a night vigil in four watches, each prahar with its own offering, and this stotra is built to carry the devotee through them. Drawn from the Shiva Purana, the महाशिवरात्रि स्तोत्रम् runs eight verses, about six minutes, and is meant specifically for that long night of worship rather than ordinary daily use. Chanted through the vigil it is said to please Shiva, to wash away sins stored up across lifetimes, and to loosen the hold of the birth-and-death cycle toward liberation. Mondays carry it too, but its true occasion is the one night named for it.
mahagauri-stotram: Mahagauri is the Mother washed white again — the dust of severe tapasya for Shiva rinsed from her until she shines pure as moonlight, worshipped on the eighth day of Navratri. The महागौरी स्तोत्रम्, eleven verses and about five minutes, sings to that purity, and tradition makes a striking claim for it: sins fall away by remembrance alone, smarana-matra, before the recitation is even finished. Devotees ask through it for saubhagya, the bliss of a steady married life, and the removal of every affliction that troubles a home. It promises, finally, both moksha and sarvartha-siddhi — the fulfilment of every aim. Mondays, Tuesdays and Fridays suit it; Navratri is its festival.
mahakal-stotram: At Ujjain stands Mahakaleshwar, the Jyotirlinga where Shiva is worshipped as Lord of Time itself, and to recite this stotra is reckoned equal to standing at that shrine. The श्री महाकाल स्तोत्रम् — twelve verses, about nine minutes, from the Shiva Purana — calls on the Mrityunjaya aspect, the conqueror of death, and on the fearless grace of Bhairava beside him. Its central plea is protection from untimely death and from accidents, and victory over time and every obstacle that time throws up. It is said to destroy all sins and grant liberation. Mondays are its day, with Maha Shivaratri and the Shravan Somvars its seasons of greatest power.
mahakali-stotram: First among the ten Mahavidyas, ten-armed and terrible, Kali devours delusion the way fire devours dry grass. The महाकाली स्तोत्रम् from the Brahmavaivarta Purana invokes her in this primal form across fifteen verses, about ten minutes. It is a fierce prayer for fierce trouble — enemies, hostile forces, the residue of black magic, all said to be scattered by it — and for protection against untimely death, evil spirits and supernatural threat of every kind. Where great delusion, mahamoha, clouds the mind, the hymn is invoked to cut through it toward spiritual awakening. It brings fearlessness, wealth and the lifting of sorrow, and for the devoted, parama gati, the ultimate liberation. Tuesdays and Saturdays carry it; Kali Puja, Diwali and Navratri are its festivals.
mahalakshmi-ashtakam: The Padma Purana sets a graded promise on this hymn, scaled to how often you recite it: once a day clears sins, twice draws wealth and grain, three times routs even great enemies. The महालक्ष्मी अष्टकम् runs eight verses and about six minutes, addressing Mahalakshmi in her full splendour. Said with real devotion it is held to confer all the siddhis, and — the line that matters most — both bhukti and mukti together, worldly enjoyment in this life and liberation beyond it. Friday is its day and Diwali its festival, but its short length makes it an easy daily companion, recited to keep the Goddess's eternal grace close at hand.
makar-sankranti-vrat-katha: Makar Sankranti marks the sun's northward turn into Capricorn, the cold beginning to break, and this Vrat Katha is the story read on that day. Drawn from the Surya Purana, the मकर संक्रांति व्रत कथा runs twelve verses, about ten minutes. It is recited to please Surya Dev and ease bodily ailments, and — since the sun's son is Shani — to pacify Shani dosha and blunt the malefic pull of Saturn. The day's customs run through it: the giving of til and gur, said to draw prosperity and abundance, and the sacred bathing and charity that yield manifold punya. It is also a katha for mending families, strengthening bonds and healing relationships gone cold. Sunday, the sun's own day, is its proper time.
mallikarjuna-stotram: Srisailam holds the second of the twelve Jyotirlingas, where Shiva is Mallikarjuna and Parvati is Bhramaramba, and reciting this hymn is counted equal to the pilgrimage there. The श्री मल्लिकार्जुन स्तोत्रम्, ten verses from the Shiva Purana and about seven minutes long, makes large claims for itself — the wiping away of brahma-hatya and every major sin, and merit equal to a crore of yajnas performed. Through it devotees ask for the clearing of obstacles, steady spiritual progress, and the fulfilment of their heart's wishes. Mondays suit it, with Maha Shivaratri and the Shravan Somvars its high days. For the sincere, the end of the road is Shivaloka.
mangal-chandika-stotram: At its center sits one of the most-recited verses in all of Devi worship — Sarva Mangala Mangalye, 'auspicious among the auspicious' — lifted from the Devi Mahatmya of the Markandeya Purana. The मङ्गलचण्डिका स्तोत्रम् gathers seven verses around this benediction, about four minutes in all, addressing the Mother in her Chandika form, gentle and welfare-giving rather than wrathful. It is recited to drive off inauspiciousness and evil influence, and to hold a person steady through hard passages and transitions, when one chapter is ending and the next has not yet found its shape. Tuesdays and Fridays are its days, with Navratri and Durga Ashtami its festivals.
mangal-dosh-nivaran-stotram: Manglik dosha — the affliction of Mars in a birth chart — is blamed for delayed marriages and strained matches, and this stotra is the devotional remedy turned to for it. Hanuman, himself ruled by Mars, is its deity; the मंगल दोष निवारण स्तोत्रम् draws on Jyotish tradition across ten verses, about seven minutes. It works to soften the malefic effects of the planet, to clear the obstacles Mars throws across marriage, and to bless a couple with a harmonious life together. Beyond the chart, it steadies the heart — calling up courage where fear and anxiety have crept in. Tuesday, the day of both Hanuman and Mars, is when it is best recited, with Hanuman Jayanti its festival.
mantra-pushpanjali: Almost every puja ends the same way: flowers cupped in the hands, the Mantra Pushpanjali chanted, the petals released at the deity's feet. The मन्त्र पुष्पाञ्जलि is that closing offering, eleven verses compiled from the Yajurveda and Rigveda, about seven minutes long. Its Vedic hymns reach not one god but many at once — Vishnu, Brahma, Shiva, Indra, Kubera — asking for prosperity, sovereignty, and the fulfilment of every wish. It is what gives a puja or havan its proper Vedic conclusion, and to recite it is said to earn merit equal to performing an elaborate yajna, sealing the whole rite in auspiciousness and divine grace. There is no fixed day for it; it belongs wherever worship ends.
manyu-suktam: Manyu is divine wrath — not blind anger but the righteous fury that drives a warrior forward — and this Rigvedic hymn (10.83–84) makes it a god to be invoked. The मन्यु सूक्तम् runs seven verses, about six minutes, and is the old chant of fighters and of anyone facing odds. It calls down valor, courage and the will to stand and fight, and is recited to scatter enemies, obstacles and opposing forces alike. Tradition leans on it for victory in legal disputes and conflicts, and for the burning-out of fear and cowardice from the heart, leaving invincible strength in their place. Tuesday, the day of Mars, is when warriors take it up.
markandeya-stuti: The boy-sage Markandeya was fated to die at sixteen; clinging to the Shiva linga as Yama's noose fell, he was spared, and Shiva became Mrityunjaya, conqueror of death, for his sake. The मार्कण्डेय स्तुति carries that story forward as a prayer against akaal mrityu, untimely death — ten verses, about seven minutes, from the Markandeya and Shiva Puranas. Parents recite it to shield children from danger and illness; everyone recites it to dissolve the fear of death entirely and to ask for long life and health. It pleases Shiva in his Mrityunjaya form and leaves courage where dread had been. Mondays are its day, with Maha Shivaratri and Pradosh its occasions.
maruti-stotra: Samartha Ramdas Swami, the seventeenth-century Marathi saint and tireless devotee of Hanuman, composed this stotra, and it remains central to the Marathi Hanuman tradition. Maruti is Hanuman's old name — son of the wind — and the मारुति स्तोत्र, eleven verses and about six minutes, asks him for the gifts he embodies: intelligence, strength, fame, courage, fearlessness and health. It is valued especially for vak-patutva, eloquence of speech, and for clearing the mental dullness that gets in the way of it. Recited faithfully it is said to drive calamity and fear out of a life and to earn the lasting grace of Maruti. Tuesdays and Saturdays are its days, with Hanuman Jayanti its festival.
matangi-stotram: Matangi is the ninth Mahavidya, Saraswati turned tantric — Goddess of speech, poetry, music and every art, worshipped in her Ucchishta form that steps clean past the conventional rules of purity and impurity. The मातंगी स्तोत्रम्, drawn from the Tantrasara, runs eight verses, about five minutes. Its great gift is vak siddhi: the state in which whatever the devotee speaks comes true, which makes it a hymn artists, musicians, writers and scholars keep close. She grants victory in debate and argument, and a charisma that draws and enchants all who come near. Wednesdays and Fridays are its days; Navratri and Vasant Panchami its festivals.
meenakshi-stotram: In Madurai, the great temple belongs not to Shiva but to the Goddess — Meenakshi, the fish-eyed queen, with Sundareshwara at her side. The श्री मीनाक्षी स्तोत्रम् from the Halasya Mahatmya brings that temple home, its recitation counted equal to worship within those very walls. Eleven verses, about eight minutes. Devotees turn to it above all for saubhagya, the lasting bliss of marriage, and for the fulfilment of wishes for a happy home and family. It carries too the gifts the Goddess is known for — beauty, grace, charm — and the full blessing of Parvati in her Meenakshi form. Fridays and Tuesdays suit it, with Navratri and the Meenakshi Kalyanam its festivals. For the sincere, it opens the way to Devi-loka.
